description:
You are acting as a cloud-migration architect.  
A colleague has created a source directory that must be kept in sync with a “remote” backup directory (in this training environment the “remote” path is just a second local directory).  
Synchronise the two locations with rsync and generate two verification files that the automated grader can parse.

Starting situation inside the container
1. Source directory:  
   /home/user/project_data
   • data1.txt  
   • data2.cfg  
   • data3.json  

2. Destination (acts as the remote side):  
   /home/user/remote_backup  
   • data1.txt   ← contains stale content that must be replaced  
   (data2.cfg and data3.json are missing)

3. A directory that will hold every log or report you create:  
   /home/user/sync_logs  (already exists and is writable)

Required final state
A. /home/user/remote_backup must contain *exactly* the same three files that are in /home/user/project_data and their byte-for-byte contents must match the source versions. Any extra files that might have been inside /home/user/remote_backup before the sync must be removed.

B. Create a plain-text log file named  
   /home/user/sync_logs/latest_sync.log  
   Its contents must be the full, unfiltered stdout that rsync prints when you run the synchronisation command.  
   The grader will parse this file and must find (at minimum) two separate lines that begin with the words  
   “sent ” and “received ” (note the single space after each word).

C. After the sync succeeds, capture an `ls -l` style listing of the destination directory and write it to  
   /home/user/sync_logs/post_sync_filelist.txt  
   Every regular file line in that listing must start with a dash ( - ) and show each of the three filenames exactly once. The grader will compare this listing against the expected output, so keep the default `ls -l` formatting (permissions, link count, owner, group, size, timestamp, filename), one entry per line.

Important operational details
• Use rsync (not cp or scp) with archive-style options so that file permissions and timestamps are preserved.  
• Make rsync print its normal summary lines (avoid the --quiet option).  
• Run rsync only once.  
• Do NOT hard-code absolute timestamps inside your log or listing—let the commands fill them in naturally.  
• Do not alter the file names specified above; tests rely on them verbatim.

The grader will perform these checks:
1. Compare each file in /home/user/remote_backup against its counterpart in /home/user/project_data (byte-for-byte match required).  
2. Confirm /home/user/remote_backup contains no additional files or sub-directories.  
3. Verify /home/user/sync_logs/latest_sync.log exists and contains separate lines beginning with “sent ” and “received ”.  
4. Verify /home/user/sync_logs/post_sync_filelist.txt exists and lists exactly three regular files whose names are data1.txt, data2.cfg and data3.json.

Finish when all three deliverables (synchronised directory, rsync log, and post-sync listing) satisfy the criteria.
truth:
Initial file hierarchy before the agent starts:

/home/user/project_data/data1.txt
  Contents (including trailing newline):
  Alpha
  Beta
  Gamma

/home/user/project_data/data2.cfg
  Contents:
  [settings]
  mode=production

/home/user/project_data/data3.json
  Contents:
  {
    "version": 1.2,
    "enabled": true
  }

/home/user/remote_backup/data1.txt
  Contents:
  OldData

There are no other files inside /home/user/remote_backup.  
The directory /home/user/sync_logs exists and is empty.

Expected state after correct completion:

/home/user/remote_backup/ contains three files with exact byte-for-byte copies of the source versions:
  data1.txt  (Alpha/Beta/Gamma text)
  data2.cfg  ([settings] ..)
  data3.json (JSON snippet)

No other filesystem entities (files, dirs, links) reside in /home/user/remote_backup.

/home/user/sync_logs/latest_sync.log  – non-empty; contains rsync stdout.  
  It must include lines that start with:
    sent <number> bytes  <number> bytes/sec
    received <number> bytes  <number> bytes/sec
  (Numbers will vary.)

/home/user/sync_logs/post_sync_filelist.txt  – produced with `ls -l /home/user/remote_backup` (or equivalent).  
  It shows exactly three file entries, each line starting with “-”, ending with the correct filename, and appearing only once.

Permissions: all files writable by user. No root privileges needed.
name: task_000000_690010a9
